content: When a deterministic keyword/substring signal feeds a decision, keep it precision-first and treat it as a candidate-surfacing HINT, not a classifier. Don't chase semantic edge cases (negation, contrast, directionality like added-vs-demanded content) with ever-more-baroque regex — those need NLU; defer them to the model and document the limitation in code + the skill doc.
context: T-030 / ISS-001: added a `fabrication` theme to prompt-engineering-improve's THEME_KEYWORDS. A Codex adversarial-review loop found ~8 successive phrasing edge cases (broad 'unsupported' → criteria-problem collision → 'inventory'/'made up of' substring collisions → criteria-vs-fabrication directionality → negation 'no fabrication' → compound-clause over-suppression → contrastive 'should not invent but did'). Each fix surfaced a narrower case; the oscillation (too broad ↔ too narrow ↔ 'use regex') was the tell that the substring mechanism — whose own docstring says 'NOT a classifier' — had hit its inherent limit. Converged by tuning for precision (word-boundary verbs + clause/position negation) and documenting that the model confirms the theme against the verdict text.
title: A keyword/substring tally can't classify negation or directionality — stop tuning, let the model confirm
